title: Covariant Onsager and Onsager-Machlup principles for active and inertial dynamics
authors: Kento Yasuda, Bin Zheng, Zhongqiang Xiong, Zhanglin Hou, Kenta Ishimoto, Xinpeng Xu, David Andelman, Shigeyuki Komura
abstract: The Onsager principle provides a variational route to the phenomenological equations of dissipative dynamics through the minimization of the Rayleighian. We develop a covariant formulation of the Onsager principle for active systems, ensuring geometric consistency under coordinate transformations. To further incorporate thermal fluctuations, we formulate the Onsager-Machlup principle for active systems by considering the Onsager-Machlup functional and the corresponding path probability for stochastic trajectories. Requiring that the path probability obeys the detailed fluctuation theorem, we show that the extended Onsager-Machlup theory is consistent with stochastic thermodynamics. Moreover, we incorporate inertia into the variational framework and show that the proper covariant equations follow when the covariant acceleration is held fixed during the variation.
summary: This paper addresses the mathematical inconsistency of existing variational principles for dissipative dynamics under coordinate transformations. The authors introduce a covariant formulation of the Onsager and Onsager-Machlup principles that remains invariant under point transformations. This new framework successfully integrates active (non-equilibrium) forces and inertial effects into a unified geometric structure. The resulting theory is shown to be consistent with the principles of stochastic thermodynamics and the detailed fluctuation theorem.
main_problem: Existing formulations of the Onsager and Onsager-Machlup principles lack covariance, making them inconsistent under coordinate transformations and unable to account for inertial effects in active systems.
main_result: The authors develop a covariant framework for the Onsager and Onsager-Machlup principles that ensures geometric consistency under coordinate transformations and successfully incorporates both active forces and inertial dynamics.
method: The study uses a variational approach involving the minimization of the Rayleighian and the Onsager-Machlup functional, utilizing a metric tensor and covariant acceleration to maintain invariance.
model_or_system: The framework applies to mesoscopic systems in contact with a heat bath, specifically addressing active systems (driven out of equilibrium) and inertial systems (including kinetic energy and mass). Examples include 3D active Brownian particles and 2D particles in polar coordinates.
key_observables: Non-equilibrium free energy, entropy production, path probability density, internal energy, and work/heat exchange.
important_parameters: Temperature (T), friction/resistance tensor (zeta), inertia tensor (mu), and active power/forces (X).
main_assumptions: The system is in a non-equilibrium state; meso-state variables are uniquely determined by micro-states; the heat bath is at a constant temperature; and transformations are smooth, one-to-one point transformations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows an entropy balance diagram illustrating the decomposition of system entropy into micro and meso parts, and the relationship between heat, work, and bath entropy change.
paper_structure: The paper first identifies the lack of covariance in existing principles, then develops a covariant formulation for active systems, extends this to include thermal fluctuations via the Onsager-Machlup principle, incorporates inertia using covariant acceleration, and validates the theory with specific models (active Brownian particles and polar coordinates) and thermodynamic consistency proofs.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a mathematically rigorous, coordinate-invariant way to describe the stochastic trajectories and thermodynamics of driven, dissipative, and inertial systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Entropy balance for a system coupled to a heat bath at temperature T. The meso-state of the system is described by q, while the microscopic configuration is represented by x with Hamiltonian H(x). The system entropy is decomposed as Ssys = Smic + Smes. If the system exchanges heat Q with the bath and work W with the surroundings, the bath entropy change is ∆Sbath = Q/T, and the total entropy production is given by σ = ∆Ssys + ∆Sbath.
